note: Master glossary used by pipeline.glossary to inline-gloss every jargon term in rendered reports. Each entry is plain-language, ≤40 words, with an attribution. Promote new terms here whenever the renderer flags an undefined token.

terms.TRIZ: A Russian engineering method (Theory of Inventive Problem Solving) for resolving contradictions; here applied to story structure to force a single instrument to do two opposite things at once.
terms.TRIZ contradiction: A pair of demands the same dramatic instrument must satisfy at the same moment, where satisfying one violates the other (truth vs belonging, mercy vs justice, etc.).
terms.logline: A one-or-two-sentence summary of a story, in the form: When [inciting incident], [flawed protagonist] must [goal] or [stakes].
terms.Polti: Georges Polti, who in 1895 catalogued the 36 dramatic situations every story uses (Supplication, Vengeance, Revolt, etc.). Source: Les Trente-Six Situations Dramatiques.
terms.Tobias: Ronald Tobias, who in 1993 catalogued 20 master plots (Quest, Pursuit, Underdog, Metamorphosis, etc.) used between broad archetypes and individual stories.
terms.Booker: Christopher Booker, whose The Seven Basic Plots (2004) identified seven universal narrative meta-patterns (Overcoming the Monster, Rebirth, Voyage and Return, etc.).
terms.STC: Save the Cat, a 2005 screenwriting framework by Blake Snyder; categorises Hollywood films into 10 commercial genres (Monster in the House, Buddy Love, Whydunit, etc.).
terms.Truby: John Truby, whose Anatomy of Story (2007) identifies four grand-genre archetypes (Myth, Memoir, Wisdom, Hero) underneath specific genres.
terms.Pearson archetype: Carol Pearson's 12 character templates (Hero, Lover, Sage, Magician, Ruler, Innocent, Orphan, Caregiver, Explorer, Rebel, Creator, Jester) used to type protagonists psychologically.
terms.Reagan arc shape: Six emotional arc shapes Reagan et al. identified across 1,700 novels (Rags-to-Riches, Tragedy, Man-in-Hole, Icarus, Cinderella, Oedipus). Man-in-Hole correlates strongest with virality.
terms.Egri controlling idea: Lajos Egri's term for the moral wager that organises a story; expressed as 'X leads to Y' (e.g., 'love conquers death', 'envy destroys love').
terms.JTBD: Jobs-To-Be-Done — the emotional 'job' an audience hires the story to do (e.g., feel less alone, rehearse a fear, validate a moral instinct).
terms.SDT: Self-Determination Theory (Deci and Ryan) — three psychological needs whose deprivation drives story-resonance: autonomy, competence, relatedness.
terms.EGI: Empirical Genius Index — composite score = 0.40 × embedding novelty + 0.40 × emotional-arc-shape proximity + 0.20 × premortem survival rate.
terms.Pareto front: In multi-objective ranking, the set of options where no option is better on every axis simultaneously; competing objectives never collapse to a single number.
terms.MAP-Elites: Multi-dimensional Archive of Phenotypic Elites — a quality-diversity algorithm that preserves the best concept in each (binned) corner of the design space.
terms.comp: Comparable film — a recently-released film with documented box-office or streaming performance that anchors a new project's market expectation. Used in 'X meets Y' positioning.
terms.premortem: A planning exercise that imagines the project has failed and asks why; here, 18 named failure modes a concept must survive before reaching the critic.
terms.kill-switch: A premortem rule whose failure auto-rejects the concept (no protagonist Want+Block+Transformation; passive protagonist; un-posterable logline; etc.).
terms.score band: A/B/C/D label assigned by the forced-distribution gate; A = top 10%, B = next 30%, C = next 40%, D = bottom 20%. Replaces the numeric 'XX/100' that gives false precision.
terms.forced distribution: Quantile binning that forbids the critic from blessing every concept; without it the score scale collapses and 'best' is indistinguishable from 'least bad'.
terms.anti-slop: Pattern-matching guardrail against generic LLM tics — TED-talk-articulate protagonists, summed-population audience numbers, em-dash-narrative grids, etc.
terms.adversarial persona critic: A prompt-bound LLM that scores the concept as if it were a specific industry decision-maker (Sundance acquisitions exec, Netflix VP, Black List reader).
terms.real-world anchor: A verified URL (PubMed PMID, CourtListener opinion, Wikipedia article, Google Trends slope) that grounds the concept in actual human friction, not pure invention.
terms.literary mutation: Application of a named author's signature structural move (Borges-labyrinth, Le-Guin-anthropological, Vonnegut-comedy-tragedy collapse) to the seed concept.
terms.seed package: The deterministic 25-axis combinatorial sample that anchors one concept: tension × space × Polti × Tobias × archetype × period × geography × etc.
terms.knowledge pack: The 3–6 verbatim excerpts (literary, philosophical, historical, case-report, legal, folkloric, poetic) the forge receives alongside the seed package.
terms.narrative grid: The five-row table showing a concept's Polti / Tobias / Booker / STC / Truby IDs alongside the human-readable label of each.
terms.cinema-school floor: A 10-school PASS/FAIL check against named film-school doctrines (Bazin, Eisenstein, Kuleshov, Bresson, Tarkovsky, etc.); concept must clear ≥7 to pass.
terms.characterological: Pertaining to character; not plot; the inner shape of the protagonist as opposed to the events around them.
terms.synopsis: A 3–5 paragraph plot summary covering Acts I, II, III; the ending IS revealed because investors are buying the whole product.
terms.Save the Cat beat sheet: Snyder's 15-beat structural template (Opening Image, Theme Stated, Set-up, Catalyst, Debate, Break into Two, B Story, etc.).
terms.feature film: A theatrical-release narrative film, typically 90–150 minutes; the default unit for cinema concept generation.
terms.limited series: A multi-episode story with a fixed end (4–10 episodes); not designed to recur for multiple seasons.
terms.ongoing series: A story-engine designed to generate new episodes indefinitely (case-of-the-week, central mystery, ensemble dynamic).
terms.format type: The unit of distribution: feature / limited series / ongoing series / doc feature / doc series. Tagged on every concept so the producer knows what to read for.
terms.runtime band: Coarse runtime bucket: short / feature_low (90 min) / feature_mid (110 min) / feature_high (140+ min) / limited_4_6 / limited_8_10.
terms.wound: The protagonist's origin event that shaped the flaw; the moment in their backstory that the story's contradiction will surface.
terms.want: The protagonist's surface goal — what they think they need.
terms.need: The deeper transformation the protagonist actually requires; usually the opposite of the want and visible only by the third act.
terms.flaw: The character trait that prevents the protagonist from achieving the want until the need is faced.
terms.arc: The change-trajectory across the story; the prose-articulated movement from wound through want into need.
terms.antagonist argument: The moral claim the antagonist makes that, in their own logic, is right; never a 'because evil' antagonist.
terms.antagonist method: The instrument the antagonist uses; should mirror-oppose the protagonist's same-instrument so they share the dramatic spine.
terms.self-revelation: Truby's term for the moment when the protagonist names what was hidden from them; the climax of the inner arc.
terms.McKee gap: Robert McKee's term for the difference between expectation and result inside a single beat or scene; the engine of dramatic tension.
terms.Egri premise: A single sentence in 'X leads to Y' form that names the moral wager the entire story is meant to demonstrate.
terms.irreversibility: A plot element that cannot be undone (death, public statement, lost document, pregnancy, crossed border, sealed container, spoken vow).
terms.the contradiction object: The specific physical thing the protagonist must hold or surrender that materially carries the TRIZ contradiction (a letter, a ring, a recording, a flag).
terms.specificity rubric: The 4-sub-dimension specificity check (sensory daily-life detail; contradiction-bearing object; named secondary character with values-conflict; ≤10-word protagonist line).
terms.audience-articulacy gate: Per-batch constraint that ≥30% of protagonists must be inarticulate or semi-articulate (vs the current 100% TED-talk-ready default).
terms.embedding novelty: Cosine distance between a concept's embedding and the existing-concept corpus; Goldilocks zone is 0.05–0.50 (>0.50 risks incoherence; <0.05 is derivative).
terms.compression progress: Schmidhuber's information-theoretic measure of 'interestingness' — concepts that compress training data unusually well after exposure.
terms.Conformal prediction: Statistical method for emitting a confidence interval around a point estimate without distributional assumptions.
terms.Lévy-stable: A heavy-tailed distribution family (De Vany & Walls); commercial film outcomes are Lévy-stable, not normal — so 'expected' is the wrong central tendency.
terms.hadith: A reported saying or action of the Prophet Muhammad in Islamic tradition; verified through chains of transmission (isnad).
terms.isnad: The chain of transmitters whose names authenticate a hadith report.
terms.samizdat: Self-published dissident literature passed hand-to-hand in the Soviet Union and Eastern Bloc; circumvented censorship.
terms.tateposhi: A Japanese rural term; not used in this glossary except as illustration.
terms.Pareto front collapse: The error of reducing multiple objectives to a single weighted score; loses the trade-off information the front exposes.
terms.CAT panel: Consensual Assessment Technique (Amabile 1982) — domain experts rank pairs of concepts; inter-rater alpha ≥ 0.70 required for validity.
terms.alpha (inter-rater): Krippendorff's alpha or Cohen's kappa; statistical measure of agreement between independent raters; >0.70 is the academic standard.
terms.tension: A binary moral or philosophical pair where the protagonist must hold both poles simultaneously; the engine of the contradiction.
terms.unusual space: A highly specific contextual setting (e.g., 'maternity ward in a country whose currency collapsed yesterday') that forces friction and texture.
terms.PubMed: The US National Library of Medicine's biomedical-research database; case reports of psychological/medical anomalies anchor concepts in verified human variance.
terms.CourtListener: An open legal-opinion database; provides verified power-imbalance / institutional-friction anchors with permanent URLs.
terms.Stith Thompson Motif Index: A six-volume catalogue of folklore motifs (impossible bargain, exchanged bride, etc.); seeds archetypal patterns.
terms.Aarne-Thompson tale type: A classification of folk-tale plots used alongside the Stith Thompson index.
terms.GDELT: Global Database of Events, Language, and Tone; tracks news anomalies in real-time as cultural-resonance signals.
terms.FRED: Federal Reserve Economic Data; macro-economic series for cultural-resonance weighting.
terms.TMDB: The Movie Database; open-data film metadata with verified box-office and platform numbers.
terms.Black List reader: An anonymous reader on Franklin Leonard's Black List who scores spec scripts; benchmark for forced-distribution calibration.
terms.spec script: A screenplay written on speculation (without a paid commission); how most working writers enter the market.
terms.greenlight: The formal decision to fund production; the pivot point from concept to capital.
terms.addressable market: The audience size a project can reasonably reach; deliberately removed from this redesign because summed-population estimates were fabricated.
terms.premortem-survival rate: Fraction of the 18 premortem rules a concept passes; component of EGI.
terms.ledger: Append-only record at .planning/state/PLAN_LEDGER.jsonl; one row per completed work item with plan SHA, artifacts, tests, deviations.
terms.plan-compliance loop: The pre-task / post-task / audit / pre-commit / stop-gate cycle that prevents plan-drift during multi-session execution.
terms.drift detection: The pre-task gate's check that the plan-file SHA hasn't changed since the most recent ledger row; catches user-edits to the plan mid-execution.
terms.stop-gate: The session-end check that RESUME.md is fresher than the latest ledger row; refuses to declare the session done if the operator-facing handoff is stale.
terms.ADR: Architecture Decision Record; a short markdown file documenting one major design decision with rationale and consequences.
terms.ANOMALY-NNN: A custom-rule code in scripts/lint_imports.py; named lint rules that enforce CLAUDE.md MUST/MUST NOT lines mechanically.
terms.Bazin doctrine: André Bazin's 'reality-of-the-recorded-image'; the school's check is whether the concept respects the unfaked durational truth of cinema.
terms.Eisenstein doctrine: Sergei Eisenstein's montage theory; the school's check is whether collision of images carries meaning beyond either image alone.
terms.Kuleshov effect: Lev Kuleshov's demonstration that audiences project meaning onto a face based on the cut adjacent to it; the school's check is whether the concept respects this assembly-time meaning.
terms.Bresson doctrine: Robert Bresson's 'model-not-actor'; the school's check is whether the concept can survive performance below the radar of psychological realism.
terms.Tarkovsky doctrine: Andrei Tarkovsky's 'sculpting in time'; the school's check is whether the concept's pacing has the necessary patience.
terms.Cassavetes doctrine: John Cassavetes' improvisation-as-truth; the school's check is whether the concept could be performed without a fixed line.
terms.Antonioni doctrine: Michelangelo Antonioni's anomic landscape; the school's check is whether the concept treats psychological emptiness as visible material.
terms.Kiarostami doctrine: Abbas Kiarostami's frame-within-frame and ethical viewer; the school's check is whether the concept implicates the audience.
terms.Akerman doctrine: Chantal Akerman's real-time domestic; the school's check is whether the concept gives unrushed attention to women's labour.
terms.Reygadas doctrine: Carlos Reygadas's slow-cinema natural-light; the school's check is whether the concept could survive long-takes.
